title: The Sign Test and Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

epigraph.text: A simple man believes anything, but a prudent man gives thought to his steps.
epigraph.quote: The sign test and Wilcoxon test make minimal assumptions about the underlying distribution.

content.text: When normality is doubtful, we can use tests based on signs or ranks.

content.title: Sign Test
content.content:
To test $H_0$: median $= m_0$:

1. Count observations above $m_0$ (positive signs)

2. Under $H_0$, each observation is equally likely to be above 
 or below $m_0$

3. The number of positive signs follows $\Binom(n, 0.5)$

content.text:
The sign test is extremely simple and makes minimal assumptions, but 
it uses only the signs, not the magnitudes.

content.title: Sign Test
content.problem:
Of 15 observations, 12 are above the hypothesized median. Test at 
$\alpha = 0.05$.
content.solution:
Under $H_0$: $X \sim \Binom(15, 0.5)$.

$P(X \geq 12) = P(X=12) + P(X=13) + P(X=14) + P(X=15) \approx 0.018$.

For two-tailed test: $p$-value $\approx 0.036 < 0.05$. Reject $H_0$.

content.title: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test
content.content:
For paired data or one-sample median test:

1. Compute differences from hypothesized median

2. Rank the absolute differences

3. Sum ranks of positive differences ($W^+$) and negative ($W^-$)

4. Compare $W = \min(W^+, W^-)$ to critical values

content.text:
The Wilcoxon test uses both signs and magnitudes (through ranks), 
making it more powerful than the sign test.
